meta.project: NNPHI — The Future of Public Health
meta.source: Scenario Planning Session - July 24 - Materials.pdf, Scenario Uncertainties (slides 4–16)

uncertainties.title: The shape of the public
uncertainties.question: What still holds people together enough to act on health collectively?
uncertainties.sourceLineRaw: FROM THE FRAGMENTING PUBLIC · FROM SICK CARE TO SELF-CARE

outcomes.title: Shared civic public
outcomes.description: People see health as a common responsibility and accept shared solutions.

outcomes.title: Layered publics
outcomes.description: National consensus is weak, but local and identity-based communities coordinate internally.

outcomes.title: Personalized publics
outcomes.description: People organize around individualized risks, values, and information feeds.

outcomes.title: No common public
outcomes.description: Health is treated mainly as a private responsibility and consumer choice.

uncertainties.title: Public health's permission to act
uncertainties.question: How much permission does public health have to act?
uncertainties.sourceLineRaw: FROM PUBLIC HEALTH UNDER POLITICAL FIRE · THE LOWERED SHIELD

outcomes.title: Broad permission to act
outcomes.description: Public health has durable civic support and clear legal authority.

outcomes.title: Issue-by-issue permission
outcomes.description: Authority is accepted for some threats but contested for others.

outcomes.title: Place-dependent permission
outcomes.description: Legitimacy and powers differ sharply across states and communities.

outcomes.title: Constant political resistance
outcomes.description: Public health remains a routine target and its powers are tightly constrained.

uncertainties.title: The institutional base
uncertainties.question: What kind of institutional base can public health sustain?
uncertainties.sourceLineRaw: FROM THE GREAT SUBTRACTION · FUNDING WHIPLASH & THE AUSTERITY CLIFF

outcomes.title: Strong public backbone
outcomes.description: Core institutions have durable funding, staff, and infrastructure.

outcomes.title: Lean core, shared capacity
outcomes.description: Small institutions rely on networks, pooled funds, and shared services.

outcomes.title: Strong nodes, weak gaps
outcomes.description: Capable organizations coexist with major geographic and functional holes.

outcomes.title: Hollowed and outsourced
outcomes.description: Critical functions move to vendors or disappear as public capacity shrinks.

uncertainties.title: The workforce model
uncertainties.question: Who does the work of public health?
uncertainties.sourceLineRaw: FROM THE WORKFORCE CLIFF · THE POWER OF ONE

outcomes.title: Rebuilt public workforce
outcomes.description: A broader multidisciplinary workforce is recruited and retained.

outcomes.title: Small ai-augmented core
outcomes.description: Lean teams work through automation, chws, and shared specialists.

outcomes.title: Distributed partner workforce
outcomes.description: Contractors, community organizations, universities, and creators do more of the work.

outcomes.title: Chronic shortage and memory loss
outcomes.description: Vacancies persist and capability resets with each departure.

uncertainties.title: The threat environment
uncertainties.question: What kind of threat environment does public health face?
uncertainties.sourceLineRaw: FROM CLIMATE AS A HEALTH MULTIPLIER · THE CHANGING NATURE OF DISEASE · THE LOWERED SHIELD

outcomes.title: Adaptation keeps pace
outcomes.description: Known climate and disease pressures grow, but investments contain many impacts.

outcomes.title: Compounding known threats
outcomes.description: Heat, smoke, disasters, outbreaks, and displacement increasingly arrive together.

outcomes.title: Slow-moving systemic crisis
outcomes.description: AMR, chronic disease, and environmental exposure create sustained strain.

outcomes.title: Unfamiliar biological shock
outcomes.description: A novel or engineered threat falls outside existing assumptions.

uncertainties.title: What public health can see
uncertainties.question: How visible is population health in real time?
uncertainties.sourceLineRaw: FROM DATA MODERNIZATION & REAL-TIME SURVEILLANCE · THE SHADOW HEALTHCARE SYSTEM

outcomes.title: Live public health picture
outcomes.description: Integrated systems provide a trusted, real-time view of population health.

outcomes.title: Connected local pictures
outcomes.description: Federated systems share enough data to coordinate while remaining decentralized.

outcomes.title: Private platforms see more
outcomes.description: Companies have faster and richer data than public agencies.

outcomes.title: Growing blind spots
outcomes.description: Fragmented systems and off-grid care leave public health partially blind.

uncertainties.title: AI infrastructure and access
uncertainties.question: Who controls and benefits from public-health AI?
uncertainties.sourceLineRaw: FROM AI AS PUBLIC HEALTH INFRASTRUCTURE

outcomes.title: Public AI utility
outcomes.description: Trusted, governed tools are available across the field.

outcomes.title: Shared network services
outcomes.description: Institutions pool AI capabilities, talent, and standards.

outcomes.title: Vendor-owned stack
outcomes.description: Agencies rent closed tools and infrastructure from private firms.

outcomes.title: Unequal AI arms race
outcomes.description: Well-resourced actors accelerate while others fall further behind.

uncertainties.title: Control of body data
uncertainties.question: Who owns and controls data from people's bodies?

outcomes.title: Trusted public use
outcomes.description: Strong governance allows biodata to support population health.

outcomes.title: Individual control
outcomes.description: People decide who can use their data and may trade or license it.

outcomes.title: Corporate control
outcomes.description: Platforms and data brokers hold the most valuable datasets.

outcomes.title: Government access
outcomes.description: Public agencies or law enforcement gain broad access for public or political purposes.

uncertainties.domain: Ability to Speak and Be Believed
uncertainties.title: Where trust lives
uncertainties.question: Who do people trust to guide health decisions?
uncertainties.sourceLineRaw: FROM THE TRUST RECESSION · THE POWER OF ONE

outcomes.title: Institutional recovery
outcomes.description: Public institutions regain trust through performance, transparency, and accountability.

outcomes.title: Trust goes local
outcomes.description: Clinicians, community health workers, and local leaders become the trusted layer.

outcomes.title: Trust moves to platforms
outcomes.description: People rely on brands, influencers, and personal AI for health guidance.

outcomes.title: Identity-bound trust
outcomes.description: People accept guidance mainly from sources aligned with their group.

uncertainties.domain: Ability to Speak and Be Believed
uncertainties.title: How health truth is established
uncertainties.question: How do people decide what health information is real?
uncertainties.sourceLineRaw: FROM THE EPISTEMIC CRISIS · THE OUTSOURCED MIND

outcomes.title: Verified evidence layer
outcomes.description: Authentication and provenance restore a broadly shared factual baseline.

outcomes.title: Human-ai mediation
outcomes.description: Trusted people and supervised AI translate evidence for different audiences.

outcomes.title: Competing evidence systems
outcomes.description: Communities use different experts, facts, and standards of proof.

outcomes.title: Synthetic fog
outcomes.description: False and real content become too difficult to distinguish at scale.

uncertainties.title: The model of prevention
uncertainties.question: How does society try to stay healthy?
uncertainties.sourceLineRaw: FROM CHRONIC, METABOLIC & LONGEVITY RECKONING · FROM SICK CARE TO SELF-CARE

outcomes.title: Healthier conditions first
outcomes.description: Environments, food systems, housing, and social supports become the main intervention.

outcomes.title: Medicine-led prevention
outcomes.description: Drugs, diagnostics, and clinical monitoring manage risk at scale.

outcomes.title: Consumer optimization
outcomes.description: Wellness products, personal data, supplements, and biotech define prevention.

outcomes.title: Personal responsibility regime
outcomes.description: Health is moralized as an individual choice regardless of structural conditions.

uncertainties.title: The structure of care
uncertainties.question: How connected is care outside hospitals to the public system?
uncertainties.sourceLineRaw: FROM THE DECENTRALIZATION OF CARE · THE SHADOW HEALTHCARE SYSTEM · THE FRINGE ASCENDANT

outcomes.title: Distributed and connected
outcomes.description: Home, retail, community, and public systems share data and responsibility.

outcomes.title: Platform-led but regulated
outcomes.description: Large platforms organize care under common rules and data standards.

outcomes.title: Parallel systems with bridges
outcomes.description: Formal and alternative systems remain distinct but connect at key points.

outcomes.title: Invisible shadow system
outcomes.description: Large portions of care sit outside oversight, surveillance, and safeguards.

uncertainties.title: Individual and movement power
uncertainties.question: Who can shape health behavior and policy at scale?
uncertainties.sourceLineRaw: FROM THE POWER OF ONE · THE FRINGE ASCENDANT

outcomes.title: Many local problem-solvers
outcomes.description: Individuals and small groups use new tools to create useful public goods.

outcomes.title: Trusted super-connectors
outcomes.description: A small number of credible people become key partners and distribution nodes.

outcomes.title: Influencers rival institutions
outcomes.description: Reach and identity matter as much as expertise or formal authority.

outcomes.title: High-leverage disruption
outcomes.description: One actor or small group can rapidly destabilize health behavior or systems.
